ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pineapple fried rice or vegetarian pineapple fried rice; FOODON_00002694 (fried rice food product) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJQ (Fried rice) is the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for Thai-style vegetarian pineapple fried rice in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pineapple fried rice or vegetarian fried rice dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of vegetarian pineapple fried rice served in a pineapple shell, approximately 500 g, as typically offered at a hawker centre or casual Thai restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food — Fried Rice (Vegetable) and USDA FoodData Central — Pineapple Fried Rice composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input values (energy 150 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 5.6 g, carbohydrate 22.0 g, sodium 240 mg per 100 g) are consistent with HPB Singapore data for vegetable fried rice and USDA FoodData Central composite data for Thai pineapple fried rice. Values are retained and cross-validated against HPB vegetable fried rice entry and USDA SR Legacy fried rice with vegetables. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where not reported in the primary sources for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Jasmine rice, white, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai vegetarian pineapple fried rice. GI of 72 is estimated using jasmine white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate base, consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Am J Clin Nutr). Pineapple and cashew nuts contribute minor GI modulation; the dish GI is conservatively anchored to the jasmine rice GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 110 g carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = 79.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 240 mg per 100 g (1200 mg per standard 500 g serving), approaching 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal, with limited potassium and fibre offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and seasoning sauce to lower sodium content, increase the vegetable proportion to boost potassium and fibre, and treat this dish as an occasional rather than daily meal choice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI jasmine rice base (GI ~72) with 22 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 1.6 g dietary fibre per 100 g, yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 79 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request brown rice substitution where available, increase cashew nuts and vegetables to slow glucose absorption, and avoid added sugar and raisins to reduce the post-meal glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.0 g per 100 g; trans fat is negligible at 0.02 g per 100 g; cashew nuts contribute heart-healthy unsaturated fats; cholesterol is 0 mg per 100 g in this vegetarian preparation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is relatively low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, making it acceptable for cardiovascular disease management when sodium is controlled; individuals should request reduced oil and soy sauce and choose a moderate portion of approximately 300 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pineapple contributes bromelain with anti-inflammatory properties; curry powder contains turmeric; cashew nuts provide magnesium and unsaturated fats; saturated fat is low at 1.0 g per 100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is broadly suitable for rheumatoid arthritis management; individuals should request brown rice to reduce glycaemic load-driven inflammation and minimise added sugar and raisins to limit refined sugar intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Pineapple Fried Rice (素菠萝炒饭; Thai: ข้าวผัดสับปะรดมังสวิรัติ) is a Thai-origin dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and casual Thai restaurants. It is prepared by stir-frying cooked jasmine rice in a wok with fresh pineapple chunks, cashew nuts, mixed vegetables (such as carrots, peas, and corn), raisins, curry powder, soy sauce, and vegetable oil, then served in a hollowed-out pineapple shell for presentation. The dish delivers a balance of savoury, sweet, and mildly spiced flavours with contrasting textures from the soft rice, juicy pineapple, and crunchy cashews. Nutritionally, it provides 150 kcal per 100 g with 22 g carbohydrate, 3 g protein, and 5.6 g fat. The jasmine rice base is a high-GI refined carbohydrate (GI ~72), making the dish a high glycaemic load meal at approximately 79 GL per standard 500 g serving. Sodium at 240 mg per 100 g is moderate but accumulates significantly at full serving size. Vitamin C at 8 mg per 100 g is contributed by pineapple and vegetables. The dish is vegetarian, dairy-free, and cholesterol-free, but contains tree nuts (cashew) and soy allergens. It is not recommended for individuals with type 2 diabetes in large portions due to the high glycaemic load, and sodium management is advised for those with hypertension.
common_user_queries: Calories in vegetarian pineapple fried rice
Is pineapple fried rice suitable for diabetics?
Pineapple fried rice GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values sourced from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with HPB Singapore vegetable fried rice data and USDA FoodData Central composite data for Thai pineapple fried rice. Values have been cross-validated against approved sources and retained. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to jasmine rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are not available in the primary approved sources for this dish type and are set to -1. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level entry exists in HPB or USDA for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles, mineral breakdown, and moisture data for this Thai-style vegetarian pineapple fried rice dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. 2. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg per 100 g; this is a confirmed zero for this vegetarian preparation containing no animal-derived ingredients (eggs omitted in this variant), consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for vegetable fried rice. 3. Trans fat at 0.02 g per 100 g is a trace level from vegetable oil used in stir-frying; this is a confirmed trace value. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Thai vegetarian pineapple fried rice was found in approved sources. 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient evidence source as it is not on the approved source list; HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central were used instead. 6. Ethnic tags 'Curry' and 'Pad Thai' from the input are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded from the output. 7. The dish is served in Singapore as a Thai-origin preparation; multilingual names cover English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai as required by the cuisine rules for a Thai dish served in Singapore.
